params.name: admin_id
params.description: The ID of the admin who is linking their Tesla account. Must be unique per admin.

request.docs:
Step 1 of the Tesla OAuth flow.

Returns a Tesla login URL that the admin must visit in their browser.
The URL includes a PKCE code challenge and a state token (stored in memory server-side).

Expected response:
{
  "url": "https://auth.tesla.com/oauth2/v3/authorize?..."
}

Open the returned URL in a browser, log in with the Tesla account, and approve access.
Tesla will redirect to the TESLA_REDIRECT_URI with ?code=...&state=... appended.

params.name: code
params.description: The authorization code Tesla appends to the redirect URL after the admin approves access.

params.name: state
params.description: The opaque state token from the Tesla redirect. Must match the one generated in Step 1 (PKCE anti-CSRF).

request.docs:
Step 2 of the Tesla OAuth flow. Normally called automatically by Tesla's redirect — you do not need to call this manually.

This handler:
1. Validates the state token (anti-CSRF check)
2. Exchanges the authorization code for access + refresh tokens using PKCE
3. Encrypts tokens with AES-256-GCM and saves them to the database
4. Syncs the admin's vehicles from the Tesla API into the DB

Expected response:
{
  "message": "Tesla account linked successfully",
  "vehicles_synced": 2
}

params.name: admin_id
params.description: The ID of the admin whose linked vehicles to list.

request.docs:
Lists all Tesla vehicles linked to an admin account (read from the database — no live Tesla API call).

IMPORTANT: The 'id' field in each vehicle object is the INTERNAL database ID.
Use this 'id' as the :vehicleID path parameter in all subsequent battery/charging requests.
Do NOT use Tesla's external vehicle_id field.

Expected response:
[
  {
    "id": 1,
    "admin_id": "admin-1",
    "vehicle_id": 1234567890,
    "vin": "5YJ3E1EA1NF000001",
    "display_name": "My Model 3"
  }
]

params.name: admin_id
params.description: The ID of the admin who owns this vehicle.

request.docs:
Fetches LIVE battery status directly from the Tesla API and saves a snapshot to the database.

REPLACE '1' in the URL with the internal DB vehicle id from 'List Tesla Vehicles'.

This call wakes the vehicle if asleep (costs ~$0.02 per wake at Tesla API rates).
If the car is asleep and cannot be woken, returns HTTP 503.

Returned fields include:
- battery_level (0-100)
- battery_range (miles)
- charging_state (Charging / Complete / Disconnected / Stopped)
- charge_rate (mph)
- charger_voltage
- charger_actual_current
- charge_limit_soc
- time_to_full_charge (hours)
- charge_energy_added (kWh)

params.name: start_date
params.description: Start of the time range (RFC3339 / ISO8601 UTC).

params.name: end_date
params.description: End of the time range (RFC3339 / ISO8601 UTC).

request.docs:
Returns time-series battery snapshots from the database for a given vehicle and date range.

REPLACE '1' in the URL with the internal DB vehicle id from 'List Tesla Vehicles'.

Snapshots are created each time 'Get Live Battery Status' is called.
Data older than 90 days is automatically pruned by POST /tesla/admin/prune.

params.name: start_date
params.description: Start of the time range (RFC3339 / ISO8601 UTC).

params.name: end_date
params.description: End of the time range (RFC3339 / ISO8601 UTC).

params.name: limit
params.description: Maximum number of charging sessions to return. Default is 50.

request.docs:
Returns historical charging sessions from the database.

REPLACE '1' in the URL with the internal DB vehicle id from 'List Tesla Vehicles'.

Charging sessions are inferred from battery snapshots: the service detects transitions
in the charging_state field (e.g. Disconnected → Charging → Complete) across consecutive
snapshots to construct session records with start/end times and energy added.

A session with no ended_at means the car is currently charging.

Deletes battery snapshots and charging logs older than 90 days from the database.

This is the data retention enforcement endpoint. Call it periodically (e.g. daily via cron)
to keep the database from growing unbounded.

Expected response:
{
  "message": "Pruned successfully",
  "snapshots_deleted": 142,
  "charging_logs_deleted": 8
}
